symbols: apple in Adam’s hand — the forbidden fruit, the act of disobedience
Eve reaching for fruit on the tree — yielding to the serpent’s temptation
fig leaves — the first covering of shame after the Fall
serpent coiled around the tree trunk — Satan as the cunning deceiver
deer in the background — the peaceful animal kingdom before the curse
lush green foliage — the abundance of Eden before sin entered
description: Two nude figures stand before a fruit-laden tree against a backdrop of dense green foliage. The man on the left holds a round fruit (apple) near his mouth, while the woman on the right reaches up to pluck another fruit from the tree with one hand. A serpent is visible winding along the tree trunk. Both figures wear small sprigs of fig leaves. A deer with antlers rests in the foliage at the lower left. The sky is clear blue above the green canopy.
theological_description: The Fall of Man depicts the decisive moment of Genesis 3:6 when Eve, deceived by the serpent, takes the forbidden fruit and shares it with Adam who is with her. This single act of disobedience shattered the covenant relationship between God and humanity, introducing sin, shame, and death into the created order (Romans 5:12). Eve’s reaching for the fruit represents yielding to the serpent’s lie—‘You will be like God, knowing good and evil’ (Genesis 3:5)—the primal temptation to autonomous self-determination apart from God. Adam’s willing participation compounds the tragedy, for as the federal head of humanity, his sin implicates all his descendants (Romans 5:19). The fig leaves represent the futile human attempt to cover shame through self-effort (Genesis 3:7). Yet even in judgment, God provides covering (Genesis 3:21) and promises the seed of the woman who will crush the serpent’s head (Genesis 3:15)—the protoevangelium that points to Christ.

scripture_refs.reason: Eve taking the fruit and giving it to Adam, the exact moment depicted
The serpent’s temptation, the eating of the fruit, and the awareness of nakedness
Through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin
The protoevangelium—the seed of the woman will crush the serpent’s head
Adam was not deceived, but the woman was deceived and became a transgressor
